findings.ai_reason: Behavior: runs a PowerShell Invoke-WebRequest to download a ZIP from a repository URL and extract it locally. Why: this is a normal installer/bootstrap pattern for a repo setup script, not evidence of exfiltration or arbitrary command execution beyond fetching files. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri '%ZIP_URL%' -OutFile $zipFile and Expand-Archive in scripts/auto-start-windows.bat line 79.

Behavior: downloads the latest Node.js LTS MSI from nodejs.org to a temp file. Why: fetching an installer from the official Node.js domain is a standard setup action for this repo type and does not by itself indicate malicious behavior. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile '%NODE_INSTALLER%' in scripts/auto-start-windows.bat line 181.

Behavior: fetches Node.js release metadata from nodejs.org to determine the latest LTS version. Why: this is a benign installer helper step and not a sensitive file read or data leak. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri 'https://nodejs.org/dist/index.json' | ConvertFrom-Json in scripts/download-nodejs.ps1 line 7.

Behavior: downloads the Node.js MSI installer from nodejs.org into a temp path. Why: this is normal dependency installation behavior and does not show arbitrary code execution or exfiltration. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile $installerPath -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop in scripts/download-nodejs.ps1 line 23.

Behavior: the comment shows a curl command piped to cmd/bash for installing the script. Why: this is a common bootstrap instruction in installer comments, and the snippet is documentation rather than executed code. Evidence: REM CMD: curl -s https://bitbucket.org/.../install-windows.bat | cmd in scripts/install-windows.bat line 4.

Behavior: uses PowerShell Invoke-WebRequest to download another local installer script. Why: downloading a project script from a known base URL is a normal installer workflow, not evidence of malicious activity. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri '%BASE_URL%/auto-start-windows.bat' -OutFile '%SCRIPT_FILE%' in scripts/install-windows.bat line 32.

Behavior: the comment documents invoking the installer via irm from a URL. Why: this is a standard installation instruction and not executable code in the file itself. Evidence: usage comment with irm https://bitbucket.org/.../install.ps1 | iex in scripts/install.ps1 line 3.

Behavior: the comment documents piping a downloaded script into Invoke-Expression. Why: although risky in general, here it is only a usage comment and not an active code path in the file. Evidence: usage comment with Invoke-WebRequest ... | Invoke-Expression in scripts/install.ps1 line 4.

Behavior: downloads install-windows.bat from the project base URL. Why: this is a normal installer step for a bootstrap script and does not itself indicate abuse. Evidence: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "$BASE_URL/install-windows.bat" -OutFile $SCRIPT_FILE in scripts/install.ps1 line 32.

Behavior: the comment shows curl piped to bash for installing nvm. Why: this is a common documented installation command, not active code in the script. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash in scripts/install.sh line 4.

Behavior: the comment shows curl piped to sh for installing nvm. Why: this is documentation for a standard setup command and not an executed path in the file. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash in scripts/install.sh line 5.

Behavior: the comment shows curl piped to bash to install nvm on macOS. Why: this is a normal developer setup instruction and not evidence of malicious behavior. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash in scripts/start-mac.sh line 39.

Behavior: the comment shows curl piped to bash to install nvm on macOS. Why: this is a standard installation recommendation and not an active exploit path. Evidence: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.40.3/install.sh | bash in scripts/start-mac.sh line 60.

Behavior: imports child_process.execSync for running shell commands from Node.js. Why: this is common in setup scripts and the snippet does not show any unsafe input source or malicious command construction. Evidence: import { execSync } from 'child_process'; in setup.js line 8.

Behavior: executes a provided command string via execSync with shell enabled. Why: this is potentially risky in general, but the snippet alone does not show untrusted input or a harmful command, and setup scripts commonly use this pattern. Evidence: execSync(command, { cwd, stdio: 'inherit', shell: true }) in setup.js line 30.

Behavior: runs 'node --version' via execSync to check the installed Node version. Why: this is a benign environment check in a setup script and not arbitrary command execution. Evidence: execSync('node --version', { encoding: 'utf-8' }) in setup.js line 43.

Behavior: checks whether window.ethereum exists in the browser. Why: this is a normal Web3 dApp pattern for MetaMask detection, not a wallet directory access. Evidence: typeof window.ethereum !== 'undefined' in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 11.

Behavior: requests Ethereum accounts from the injected wallet provider. Why: this is standard wallet connection flow in a dApp and does not read local wallet files. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' }) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 24.

Behavior: creates an ethers BrowserProvider from window.ethereum and gets a signer. Why: this is normal Web3 integration code and not evidence of file access or exfiltration. Evidence: new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum) and await provider.getSigner() in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 33.

Behavior: requests the currently connected Ethereum accounts from the wallet provider. Why: this is a standard read-only wallet API call in a frontend dApp, not access to local keystore files. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_accounts' }) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 77.

Behavior: initializes a BrowserProvider and signer if one is not already present. Why: this is routine wallet state synchronization in a Web3 frontend and does not imply sensitive file access. Evidence: new ethers.BrowserProvider(window.ethereum); signer = await provider.getSigner(); in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 84.

Behavior: requests a chain switch through the wallet provider. Why: this is a normal dApp wallet interaction and not a wallet directory or keystore operation. Evidence: window.ethereum.request({ method: 'wallet_switchEthereumChain', params: [{ chainId: ... }] }) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 123.

Behavior: registers an accountsChanged event listener on window.ethereum. Why: this is standard reactive wallet integration in a frontend app and not evidence of exfiltration. Evidence: window.ethereum.on('accountsChanged', callback) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 157.

Behavior: removes the accountsChanged listener from window.ethereum. Why: this is cleanup code for a normal event subscription and not a sensitive operation. Evidence: window.ethereum.removeListener('accountsChanged', callback) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 160.

Behavior: registers a chainChanged event listener on window.ethereum. Why: this is standard Web3 frontend behavior for reacting to network changes, not wallet file access. Evidence: window.ethereum.on('chainChanged', callback) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 172.

Behavior: removes the chainChanged listener from window.ethereum. Why: this is routine cleanup for a browser wallet event listener and not suspicious by itself. Evidence: window.ethereum.removeListener('chainChanged', callback) in frontend/src/services/web3.js line 175.
